concepts.title: Check for Symmetry
concepts.conceptBody:
Check for symmetry

  1. Check whether function is even.
    1. Replace x by - x
    2. If no change in f(x), then function is even
    3. Graph is symmetrical about y-axis.
  2. Check if function is odd
    1. Replace x by - x
    2. If f (- x) = - f(x), then function is odd
    3. Graph is symmetric about origin
  3. Replace y by - y, if no change, then the graph is symmetrical about x-axis.
  4. If function is periodic, draw graph only in the period, as the remaining curve is a repetition of this one.

concepts.title: Intersection Points
concepts.conceptBody:
Intersection Points

a) Find points of intersection with $x$-axis & $y$-axis. for $\mathrm{x}$ axis, put $\mathrm{y}=0$ $\mathrm{y}$ axis, put $\mathrm{x}=0$

   

b) Special care of double \& triple roots
for example, if $f(x)=(x-a)^2 g(x)$
here $x=a$, is a double root of $f(x)$ or you can also say repeated root of $f(x)=0$
note in this case $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})=0, \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{a})=0$ but $\mathrm{f}^{\prime \prime}(\mathrm{a}) \neq 0$ i.e. can be point of local maxima / local minima now take $f(x)=(x-a)^3 g(x)$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}$ is a triple root of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ here, note
$\begin{aligned} & f(a)=0 \\ & f^{\prime}(a)=0 \\ & f^{\prime \prime}(a)=0, \text { but } \\ & f^{\prime \prime}(a) \neq 0\end{aligned}$

$\Rightarrow a$ is the point of inflection (as shown in diagram) i.e. the point where shape of the curve changes from convex to concave & vice-versa.
$\therefore \quad$ TIP: if $f(x)=(x-a)^n g(x)$
$\&$ nth derivative is the first non-zero derivative then
if $\mathrm{n}$ is even $\rightarrow \mathrm{x}=$ a is a point of local maxima / minima
$\mathrm{n}$ is odd $\rightarrow_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{a}$ is a point of inflection
let us take another example
$\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)^2$
we can see that $x=1$ is a double root of $f(x)$
Step 1: no symmetry
Step 2: $\quad$ has double root at $\mathrm{x}=1$
Step 3: $\quad$ put $f(x)>0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)^2>0$

 

\begin{gathered} \Rightarrow \quad \text { for } x \in(0, \infty) y>0 \\ x \in(-\infty, 0) y<0 \end{gathered} so approximate graph would be 

 

from this we can also deduce that $\mathrm{x}=1$ will be point of local minima & not local maxima. If it was local maxima then the curve will come in the negative half.

concepts.title: Sign Of Graph
concepts.conceptBody:
Sign Of Graph

(a) Put $y>0$ and see where the graph is above $\mathrm{x}$-axis \& where it is below $\mathrm{x}$-axis for example, if $f(x)=x(x-1)(x-2)$ put $x(x-1)(x-2)>0$

  

So, corresponding to it the graph will be above $\mathrm{x}$-axis where region is +ve & below $\mathrm{x}$-axis where region is represented by - ve sign.

  

Further $\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)(\mathrm{x}-2)$ is a polynomial function, so it will be continuous & hence the following will be the curve.  

Check the domain: Find the domain (especially in cases where denominator can become zero). Draw the curve only in its domain.

concepts.title: Asymptote
concepts.conceptBody:
Examples of asymptote

  

(a) Horizontal asymptote - If $\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=c$, where $c_1$ is finite then $\mathrm{y}=c$ is horizontal asymptote in right half. - If $\lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=c_2$, where $c_2$ is finite then $\mathrm{y}=c_2$ is horizontal asymptote in left half. For example, $y=\tan ^{-1} x$

  

here $\quad y=\pi / 2$ is a horizontal asymptote in right half. $y=-\pi / 2$ is a horizontal asymptote in left half. 

(b) Vertical Asymptote - If $\lim _{y \rightarrow \infty} \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{y})=c_1$ is finite, then $\mathrm{x}=c_1$ is a vertical asymptote OR in normal words, if for a finite value of $\mathrm{x}$, $\mathrm{y}$ approaches $(\infty$ or $-\infty)$ then $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{c}$ is a vertical asymptote. e.g.  

$\mathrm{x}=\pi / 2$, a vertical asymptote                                                   $\mathrm{x}=0$, a vertical asymptote.

 Take special care in case of (a) when denominator can be zero. Put denominator $=0$ to get the value of $x$ for which vertical asymptote will be formed. (b) Logarithm functions for $\log (f(x))=0$, find $f(x)=1 \&$ for those values of $x$ it will be a vertical asymptote.
